---
title: "Author's Note"
date: 2025-07-07
sidebar: true
---


```{=html}
<script>document.body.classList.add('book-page');</script>
```

This is not an article about Chinese artificial intelligence.


Nor is it an argument about geopolitics, export controls, benchmark leadership, or the relative
strengths of one research laboratory over another. Those discussions are important in their own right,
but they are not the subject of these pages.


This is an essay about a recurring phenomenon in the history of computing.

Every technological era begins by believing it has discovered the fundamental currency of progress. In
one generation it is transistor density. In another, processor frequency. Later it becomes the number of
cores, the size of distributed clusters, or the amount of available memory. For a time, these
relationships appear almost immutable. They are observed, measured, formalized and eventually
accepted as natural laws.


Then something curious happens.


A new generation of engineers arrives, not because they possess superior mathematics or larger
budgets, but because they ask questions that the previous generation gradually stopped asking. They
inherit systems whose assumptions have become invisible through familiarity. Their advantage is not
that they know more. It is that they no longer mistake engineering choices for the laws of physics.


The history of computing is filled with such moments.


The transition from vacuum tubes to transistors was not simply a change in materials; it was a change
in what engineers believed was possible. The decline of single-core processors did not occur because
clock speeds ceased to matter, but because another constraint—power density—quietly became more
important. The rise of cloud computing was not merely the consequence of larger datacenters, but of
recognizing that computation itself could be treated as a dynamically allocated resource rather than a
fixed asset. Again and again, the field has advanced not because yesterday's ideas were wrong, but
because yesterday's bottlenecks ceased to be tomorrow's.


Artificial intelligence now appears to be passing through a similar transition.


For much of the past decade, the dominant narrative surrounding large language models has been one
of scale. Larger datasets, larger clusters, larger models and larger computational budgets produced
undeniably remarkable progress. This observation was both empirically correct and historically
significant. Yet somewhere along the way, a useful description of how progress had occurred gradually
hardened into an assumption about how progress must continue.


That assumption is now being questioned.


The laboratories discussed throughout this essay—DeepSeek, Qwen, Moonshot AI, MiniMax and others
—did not simply build capable language models. More interestingly, they exposed a different way of
thinking about the problem itself. Rather than treating the transformer as a finished invention whose
capabilities depended primarily upon additional computation, they approached it as a complex
computational system still rich with hidden inefficiencies. Memory movement, numerical precision, distributed communication, routing algorithms, compiler optimizations and hardware utilization
became first-class research problems rather than implementation details.


Whether these particular models remain state of the art five years from now is almost beside the point.


The engineering ideas they represent almost certainly will not disappear.


If the argument presented here is correct, future historians of artificial intelligence may look back on
this period not as the moment one laboratory overtook another, nor even as the beginning of reasoning
models, but as the point at which frontier AI ceased to be viewed primarily as a machine learning
problem and began to resemble something much older: a problem in computer architecture,
distributed systems, numerical computing and the economics of computation itself.


That, ultimately, is the story this essay attempts to tell.
